In forensic toxicology, cocaine is commonly encountered in postmortem examinations or drug-related incident investigations. The compound is often detected through high-performance liquid chromatography with mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), and its metabolites—such as benzoylecgonine—are routinely measured in urine or postmortem tissue. Chronic or excessive use can induce neuroadaptive changes, including dopamine receptor downregulation, oxidative stress, and structural alterations in the prefrontal cortex and striatum. These pathophysiological responses correlate with both acute toxicity and long-term neurocognitive deficits.

The compound induces subjective effects such as euphoria, altered perception, and cognitive impairment, with documented neurophysiological correlates including diminished prefrontal cortical function and reduced working memory capacity.

In forensic pathology, exposure to cannabis is evaluated through qualitative and quantitative analysis of biological specimens—typically blood, urine, or postmortem tissue—using gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to detect metabolites such as 11-nor-9-carboxy-THC (THC-COOH). Structurally, it belongs to the phenethylamine class, with a para-methyl substitution on the phenyl ring and an extended aliphatic chain.
